election_1928.breitscheid_coalition_spea.subtitle: La Guardia is viewed as friendlier toward labor, reformers, and the left edge of the coalition.

content.content: Fiorello La Guardia has been chosen as Speaker.

election_1928.grand_coalition.title: We can form a national stabilization coalition.

content: There are enough votes to form a national stabilization coalition of the Democratic Party, Farmer-Labor, and Republican Party. Given our weak electoral performance,

content.content: and our previous attempt to bring down the governing arrangement,
and the revolt against our prior coalition leadership,

content: has asked Farmer-Labor to take the lead role in organizing the House.

options.title: We will tolerate a minority Farmer-Labor government.

content: The Farmer-Labor Speaker is likely to be Floyd B. Olson. He can hold agrarian and labor-populist votes, but he will expect real concessions on relief, farm debt, and committee influence.

content.content: Because prior budget restraint damaged the coalition, many Democrats are reluctant to participate again. Some support outside toleration to keep extremists out of power; others prefer opposition and a sharper Democratic identity.
Olson is not opposed to a coalition with us. Alternatively, we could enter a toleration agreement.
Floyd B. Olson welcomes us into the organizing coalition. Now, we have to settle the committee and department bargain.

options.title: We will tolerate Olson after all.

content: By refusing to join or tolerate the stabilization coalition, we throw the balance of Congress to the right.

content.content: There are enough votes for a conservative bloc with Republican, Liberty League, and NUSJ support, but NUSJ and the Silver Legion remain unreliable anti-system forces.
There are not enough votes for a coherent conservative majority, and NUSJ and the Silver Legion remain hostile to constitutional coalition government.

If we do not at least tolerate the arrangement, there will be new elections in a few months. In the meantime, the President and Speaker will try to govern through executive action, committee leverage, and emergency appropriations. It is not clear we will do any better in the next election.

content: There is no path to a workable Congress majority or minority government. The next elections are set for

content.content: The people's faith in democracy is shaken by this ordeal. Of course, the Silver Legion are exploiting the instability to claim that democracy is unworkable, and that the republic needs a strong leader.

election_1928.toleration_bruning.unavailableSubtitle: The parties supporting the Farmer-Labor-led arrangement do not have a majority.

content.content: We have decided to tolerate Olson's organizing arrangement. We will vote against no-confidence moves and keep the far right out of power, but we will not directly own the congressional agenda.

options.title: Thus, there is no clear majority.
Perhaps a right-wing coalition may form.
Perhaps we could ask Republican moderates to change their minds?
What are the other governing arrangements?

content.content: Unfortunately, the Republican bloc is unwilling to enter another stabilization coalition with the Democrats, so the arrangement cannot be formed.
Under the influence of Gustav Stresemann, the DVP agrees to allow us into the coalition, and so does the BVP.

options.title: But they would not have a majority without the SPD! The DNVP and Silver Legion have no interest in participating in government.
There is no chance that we would agree.
What are the other governing arrangements?

content.content: The DVP asks us to adhere to their conservative economic policies (that is, extreme austerity and labor discipline) in exchange for us joining the coalition.
We make the case that without the SPD, there is no parliamentary majority, given the DNVP and Silver Legion's hostility to constitutional government.
Somehow, we succeed in convincing them! We are now in a coalition.
As expected, the DVP is too set in their anti-democratic ways.

content: As expected, the Democratic Party is the largest party, and this time, we are expected to take the lead role in organizing the House, including selecting the Speaker. President
has asked us to assemble a working congressional majority.

content.content: As our relation with the DVP is poor, they are demanding more minister posts in order to remain part of the coalition.
Who should be Speaker of the House?

election_1928.muller.title: Sam Rayburn for Speaker
election_1928.muller.subtitle: Rayburn is a disciplined Democratic organizer and a safe choice for keeping the House in line.

content.content: Rayburn's selection as Speaker is approved by all our potential allies.

election_1928.wels.title: Henry T. Rainey for Speaker
election_1928.wels.subtitle: Rainey is a trusted House leader and an acceptable New Deal loyalist.

content.content: Rainey's selection as Speaker is approved by our potential allies.

election_1928.braun.title: John Nance Garner for Speaker
election_1928.braun.subtitle: Garner can reassure conservatives, but labor and New Deal liberals will demand procedural concessions.

content.content: Farmer-Labor has significant qualms about putting Garner in charge of the House coalition, given his hostility to aggressive redistribution. They ask for procedural concessions in exchange.

election_1928.braun_2.subtitle: John Nance Garner will be Speaker.

content.content: We have promised to let Farmer-Labor have more influence in House procedure in response to Garner's selection.

election_1928.z_speaker.title: Let Farmer-Labor have the Speaker.
election_1928.z_speaker.subtitle: Floyd B. Olson will be Speaker. This is unexpected and will cause internal dissent, but may stabilize the House.

content.content: This is highly unexpected, and many in the left and center of our party are criticizing this decision.
Floyd B. Olson of Farmer-Labor has been chosen as Speaker.

election_1928.z_speaker_wirth.title: Let Henrik Shipstead of Farmer-Labor be Speaker.
election_1928.z_speaker_wirth.subtitle: Shipstead is a Farmer-Labor progressive. This may give us a better position in negotiating House rules.

election_1928.no_govt.title: We do not form the government.
election_1928.no_govt.subtitle: This will cause significant internal dissent, and will lead to increased distrust from the conservative parties.

content: Are we sure about not participating in government? This will cause significant internal dissent, and will lead to increased distrust from the conservative parties. Even debating this option causes internal backlash. In addition, it is possible that the conservative and right-wing parties will also be unable to form a coalition government, leading to a regime appointed by President

options.title: We hope to reach a socialist majority in the next election.
It is a trap to form a government now. We would be unable to accomplish our goals.

content.content: What are our goals here, exactly?

content: Since the remaining conservative and right-wing parties cannot form a tolerable governing agreement, new elections have been called for
. The previous speaker has resigned, and has been replaced by Heinrich Brüning, also of the Center Party, ruling as part of a presidential cabinet.

content.content: We are seen as responsible for the failure to form a government, and many of our middle-class and even some working-class supporters have deserted the party.

content: We can pick from any number of ministries. Controlling a ministry allows us to directly effect policy in that area.

content.content: Any ministry that we do not take will be given to the SPA.
If the SPA controls the Defense Department Ministry, the army will most likely revolt.

Because the presidency controls the executive branch, cabinet departments stay with the president's party. The congressional coalition determines the Speaker, committee control, confirmations, and whether bills can pass; it does not hand cabinet departments to coalition partners.

election_1928.labor.subtitle: Taking the Labor Ministry is expected of us.

election_1928.foreign.subtitle: This is usually the domain of Gustav Stresemann of the DVP.

We still have some more leverage to gain ministries.
